concepts.name: System, Surroundings, and Universe
concepts.explanation: The system is the part of the universe under study; the surroundings are everything else. Together they form the universe. The boundary separates the system from its surroundings.
concepts.formula_reaction: Universe = System + Surroundings

concepts.name: Types of Systems – Open, Closed, and Isolated
concepts.explanation: An open system exchanges both energy and matter; a closed system exchanges only energy; an isolated system exchanges neither. Examples: open beaker (open), closed vessel (closed), thermos flask (isolated).

concepts.name: State Functions and State Variables
concepts.explanation: State functions (or state variables) depend only on the state of the system, not on how it was reached. Examples: pressure (p), volume (V), temperature (T), internal energy (U), enthalpy (H).

concepts.name: Internal Energy (U) as a State Function
concepts.explanation: Internal energy is the total energy (chemical, electrical, mechanical, etc.) of a system. It is a state function. For an adiabatic process (no heat exchange), the change in internal energy equals the work done: ΔU = w_ad.
concepts.formula_reaction: ΔU = U₂ - U₁ = w_ad (adiabatic)

concepts.name: Work in Thermodynamics – Pressure-Volume Work
concepts.explanation: Work done on or by a system can be calculated as w = -p_ex ΔV, where p_ex is the external pressure and ΔV is the change in volume. For compression, w is positive; for expansion, w is negative.
concepts.formula_reaction: w = -p_ex ΔV = -p_ex (V_f - V_i)

concepts.name: Reversible and Irreversible Processes
concepts.explanation: A reversible process proceeds infinitely slowly through a series of equilibrium states. An irreversible process is not in equilibrium at every stage. The maximum work is obtained in a reversible process.
concepts.formula_reaction: w_rev = -∫p dV; w_rev = -nRT ln(V_f/V_i) for isothermal reversible expansion.

concepts.name: First Law of Thermodynamics
concepts.explanation: The first law states that energy cannot be created or destroyed; it can only be converted from one form to another. Mathematically, ΔU = q + w, where q is heat added and w is work done on the system.
concepts.formula_reaction: ΔU = q + w

concepts.name: Heat (q) and its Sign Convention
concepts.explanation: Heat is energy transferred due to temperature difference. q is positive when heat is absorbed by the system (endothermic) and negative when heat is released (exothermic). At constant volume, ΔU = q_v.
concepts.formula_reaction: q = C × ΔT; ΔU = q_v (at constant volume)

concepts.name: Enthalpy (H) – Definition and Significance
concepts.explanation: Enthalpy is a state function defined as H = U + pV. It represents the heat content of a system. At constant pressure, ΔH = q_p, which is the heat absorbed or released by the system.
concepts.formula_reaction: H = U + pV; ΔH = ΔU + pΔV

concepts.name: Relation between ΔH and ΔU
concepts.explanation: For reactions involving gases, ΔH = ΔU + Δn_g RT, where Δn_g is the change in the number of moles of gaseous species. If no gases are involved, ΔH ≈ ΔU.
concepts.formula_reaction: ΔH = ΔU + Δn_g RT

concepts.name: Extensive and Intensive Properties
concepts.explanation: Extensive properties depend on the amount of substance (e.g., mass, volume, U, H). Intensive properties do not (e.g., temperature, pressure, density). Molar properties are intensive.

concepts.name: Heat Capacity (C), Specific Heat, and Molar Heat Capacity
concepts.explanation: Heat capacity is the heat required to raise the temperature by 1 K. Molar heat capacity is per mole; specific heat is per gram. q = C × ΔT = m × c × ΔT.
concepts.formula_reaction: q = C ΔT; C_m = C/n; c = C/m

concepts.name: Relation between C_p and C_v for an Ideal Gas
concepts.explanation: For an ideal gas, C_p - C_v = R. This is because at constant pressure, additional heat is required to do expansion work. ΔH = C_p ΔT; ΔU = C_v ΔT.
concepts.formula_reaction: C_p - C_v = R

concepts.name: Calorimetry – Measurement of ΔU and ΔH
concepts.explanation: Calorimetry measures heat changes in chemical reactions. At constant volume (bomb calorimeter), ΔU = q_v. At constant pressure (coffee-cup calorimeter), ΔH = q_p.

concepts.name: Standard Enthalpy of Reaction (Δ_rH°)
concepts.explanation: The standard enthalpy of reaction is the enthalpy change when a reaction occurs with all substances in their standard states (pure form at 1 bar, usually 298 K).
concepts.formula_reaction: Δ_rH° = Σ a_i Δ_fH°(products) - Σ b_i Δ_fH°(reactants)

concepts.name: Standard Enthalpy of Formation (Δ_fH°)
concepts.explanation: The enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its constituent elements in their most stable states (reference states) under standard conditions. Elements in their reference states have Δ_fH° = 0.
concepts.formula_reaction: e.g., H₂(g) + ½O₂(g) → H₂O(l); Δ_fH° = -285.8 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Standard Enthalpy of Combustion (Δ_cH°)
concepts.explanation: The enthalpy change when one mole of a substance undergoes complete combustion in oxygen under standard conditions. Combustion reactions are always exothermic (Δ_cH° negative).
concepts.formula_reaction: e.g., CH₄(g) + 2O₂(g) → CO₂(g) + 2H₂O(l); Δ_cH° = -890.3 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Standard Enthalpy of Fusion (Δ_fusH°)
concepts.explanation: The enthalpy change when one mole of a solid melts (fuses) to liquid at its melting point and standard pressure. It is positive (endothermic). For ice: Δ_fusH° = +6.0 kJ mol⁻¹ at 273 K.
concepts.formula_reaction: H₂O(s) → H₂O(l); Δ_fusH° = +6.0 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Standard Enthalpy of Vaporisation (Δ_vapH°)
concepts.explanation: The enthalpy change when one mole of a liquid vaporises (boils) at its boiling point and standard pressure. It is positive (endothermic). For water: Δ_vapH° = +40.79 kJ mol⁻¹ at 373 K.
concepts.formula_reaction: H₂O(l) → H₂O(g); Δ_vapH° = +40.79 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Standard Enthalpy of Sublimation (Δ_subH°)
concepts.explanation: The enthalpy change when one mole of a solid directly converts to vapour (sublimes) under standard conditions. It is positive (endothermic).
concepts.formula_reaction: e.g., CO₂(s) → CO₂(g); Δ_subH° = +25.2 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation
concepts.explanation: Hess's law states that the enthalpy change for a reaction is independent of the path taken; it is the sum of the enthalpy changes of the intermediate steps. It allows calculation of ΔH for reactions that cannot be measured directly.
concepts.formula_reaction: Δ_rH = Δ_rH₁ + Δ_rH₂ + Δ_rH₃ + ...

concepts.name: Enthalpy of Atomisation (Δ_aH°)
concepts.explanation: The enthalpy change when one mole of a substance is completely broken down into its constituent atoms in the gas phase. For diatomic molecules, it equals bond dissociation enthalpy. For metals, it equals sublimation enthalpy.
concepts.formula_reaction: e.g., CH₄(g) → C(g) + 4H(g); Δ_aH° = 1665 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Bond Enthalpy – Bond Dissociation and Mean Bond Enthalpy
concepts.explanation: Bond dissociation enthalpy is the energy required to break one mole of a specific bond in a gaseous molecule. For polyatomic molecules, mean bond enthalpy is the average energy required to break the same type of bond.
concepts.formula_reaction: CH₄(g) → C(g) + 4H(g); mean C-H bond enthalpy = 1665/4 = 416 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Enthalpy of Reaction from Bond Enthalpies
concepts.explanation: For gas-phase reactions, Δ_rH° can be estimated as the sum of bond enthalpies of reactants minus the sum of bond enthalpies of products (energy required to break bonds minus energy released in forming bonds).
concepts.formula_reaction: Δ_rH° = Σ bond enthalpies (reactants) - Σ bond enthalpies (products)

concepts.name: Lattice Enthalpy
concepts.explanation: Lattice enthalpy is the energy required to completely separate one mole of an ionic solid into its constituent gaseous ions. It is always positive. It can be calculated using the Born-Haber cycle.
concepts.formula_reaction: NaCl(s) → Na⁺(g) + Cl⁻(g); Δ_latH° = +788 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Born-Haber Cycle
concepts.explanation: A Born-Haber cycle is a thermodynamic cycle used to calculate the lattice enthalpy of an ionic compound by applying Hess's law to the various steps (sublimation, ionization, bond dissociation, electron gain, and lattice formation).

concepts.name: Enthalpy of Solution (Δ_solH°) and Hydration
concepts.explanation: Enthalpy of solution is the enthalpy change when one mole of a solute dissolves in a solvent. For ionic compounds, Δ_solH° = Δ_latH° + Δ_hydH° (hydration enthalpy). It can be positive (endothermic) or negative.
concepts.formula_reaction: Δ_solH° = Δ_latH° + Δ_hydH°

concepts.name: Spontaneous Process – Introduction
concepts.explanation: A spontaneous process is one that has the potential to proceed without external assistance. It does not imply rapid reaction; it depends on thermodynamics, not kinetics. Spontaneous processes are irreversible.

concepts.name: Entropy (S) – Measure of Disorder
concepts.explanation: Entropy is a state function that measures the degree of randomness or disorder in a system. The greater the disorder, the higher the entropy. ΔS = q_rev/T for a reversible process.
concepts.formula_reaction: ΔS = q_rev / T

concepts.name: Second Law of Thermodynamics
concepts.explanation: The second law states that for an isolated system, entropy always increases for a spontaneous process. In a reversible process, ΔS_total = 0. For a spontaneous process, ΔS_total > 0.
concepts.formula_reaction: ΔS_total = ΔS_sys + ΔS_surr > 0 (spontaneous)

concepts.name: Criterion for Spontaneity using Entropy
concepts.explanation: A process is spontaneous if the total entropy change (system + surroundings) is positive. For a system at constant temperature, ΔS_total = ΔS_sys - ΔH_sys/T. Spontaneous when ΔS_total > 0.
concepts.formula_reaction: ΔS_total = ΔS_sys - ΔH_sys/T > 0

concepts.name: Gibbs Energy (G) – Definition and Significance
concepts.explanation: Gibbs energy is defined as G = H - TS. The change in Gibbs energy at constant temperature is ΔG = ΔH - TΔS. It is the maximum useful work obtainable from a process at constant pressure and temperature.
concepts.formula_reaction: G = H - TS; ΔG = ΔH - TΔS

concepts.name: Spontaneity Criterion using Gibbs Energy
concepts.explanation: At constant pressure and temperature, a process is spontaneous if ΔG < 0. It is at equilibrium if ΔG = 0, and non-spontaneous if ΔG > 0.
concepts.formula_reaction: ΔG < 0 (spontaneous); ΔG = 0 (equilibrium); ΔG > 0 (non-spontaneous)

concepts.name: Effect of Temperature on Spontaneity
concepts.explanation: Spontaneity depends on the relative magnitudes of ΔH and TΔS. For ΔH < 0 and ΔS > 0, always spontaneous. For ΔH > 0 and ΔS < 0, never spontaneous. For ΔH > 0 and ΔS > 0, spontaneous at high T. For ΔH < 0 and ΔS < 0, spontaneous at low T.

concepts.name: Third Law of Thermodynamics
concepts.explanation: The third law states that the entropy of a perfectly crystalline pure substance approaches zero as the temperature approaches absolute zero (0 K). It allows calculation of absolute entropies.

concepts.name: Standard Entropy (S°) and Entropy Change of Reaction
concepts.explanation: Standard entropy (S°) is the absolute entropy of a substance in its standard state at 298 K. For a reaction, Δ_rS° = Σ a_i S°(products) - Σ b_i S°(reactants).
concepts.formula_reaction: Δ_rS° = Σ a_i S°(products) - Σ b_i S°(reactants)

concepts.name: Gibbs Energy and Equilibrium Constant
concepts.explanation: The standard Gibbs energy change is related to the equilibrium constant by Δ_rG° = -RT ln K. A large negative Δ_rG° corresponds to a large K (products favoured). Δ_rG° = -2.303 RT log K.
concepts.formula_reaction: Δ_rG° = -RT ln K = -2.303 RT log K

concepts.name: Thermochemical Equations
concepts.explanation: A thermochemical equation is a balanced chemical equation that includes the enthalpy change (Δ_rH). The physical states of all substances must be specified. The coefficients represent moles of substances.
concepts.formula_reaction: e.g., C₂H₅OH(l) + 3O₂(g) → 2CO₂(g) + 3H₂O(l); Δ_cH° = -1367 kJ mol⁻¹.
